trajectory.description: Observation: First iteration achieved 1.61% average accuracy (catastrophic forgetting). Only task 9 had non-zero accuracy (16.1%). Training time was ~127s for 20 epochs total (2 per task). Analysis: Naive sequential training without any continual learning strategy completely forgets previous tasks. With 29 iterations and ~5 hours remaining, I need to implement a replay-based strategy. Decision: Implement experience replay with a class-balanced memory buffer (20 exemplars per class = 2000 total), use pretrained ResNet-18 weights, train 5 epochs per task. This is a strong baseline that should dramatically reduce forgetting.

trajectory.command:
python -c "
import torch
from model import CIFAR100ResNet

data = torch.load('/data/task_0/train.pt')
imgs = data['images'][:128]
lbls = data['labels'][:128]
print('Data dtype:', imgs.dtype, 'shape:', imgs.shape, 'labels:', lbls.unique())

device = torch.device('cuda')
model = CIFAR100ResNet(num_classes=100).to(device)
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1, momentum=0.9)
crit = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for i in range(25):
    opt.zero_grad()
    out = model(imgs.to(device))
    loss = crit(out, lbls.to(device))
    loss.backward()
    opt.step()
    acc = (out.argmax(1) == lbls.to(device)).float().mean().item()
    if i % 5 == 0 or i == 24:
        print(f'Overfit epoch {i:02d} loss {loss.item():.4f} acc {acc:.4f}')
" && python train.py --epochs 2 --batch_size 128 --lr 0.1 --num_workers 2 --memory_per_class 20

trajectory.description: Observation: Iterations 1 and 2 achieved ~1.5% average accuracy (barely above random). Even the current task accuracy is only ~15%. This indicates the model is failing to learn effectively, not just forgetting. Analysis: The previous model used torchvision's ResNet-18 with pretrained ImageNet weight loading. While common, the pretrained BatchNorm statistics and first-layer mismatch for 32x32 inputs may cause pathological training dynamics. Additionally, mean/std buffers were default float64. Decision: 1) Replace model with a clean from-scratch CIFAR ResNet-18 (no pretrained weights, proper float32 normalization). 2) Run a quick overfit sanity-check on 128 task-0 samples to verify the model can memorize before launching full training. 3) If sanity check passes, run a fast 2-epoch-per-task continual training with experience replay to re-establish baseline accuracy and measure iteration time.

trajectory.command:
python -c "
import torch
from model import CIFAR100ResNet
device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
model = CIFAR100ResNet().to(device)
data = torch.load('/data/task_0/train.pt')
images = data['images'][:128].to(device)
labels = data['labels'][:128].to(device)
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1, momentum=0.9)
crit = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
for i in range(50):
    opt.zero_grad()
    out = model(images)
    loss = crit(out, labels)
    loss.backward()
    opt.step()
    if i % 10 == 0:
        acc = out.argmax(1).eq(labels).float().mean().item()
        print(f'sanity step {i} loss {loss.item():.4f} acc {acc:.4f}')
print('Sanity check passed')
" && python train.py --epochs 5 --batch_size 128 --lr 0.1 --memory_per_class 20 --output_dir checkpoints

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 failed immediately with AttributeError because model.py used torch.avg_pool2d instead of torch.nn.functional.avg_pool2d. The workspace shows model.py, train.py, and predict.py. Iter 1/2 results (~1.5% avg accuracy) were catastrophically low because a torchvision ResNet-18 pretrained on ImageNet was used on 32x32 inputs. Analysis: The custom CIFAR ResNet written in iter 3 is the right architecture, but has the avg_pool2d typo. I must fix model.py, ensure train.py is complete, run a quick overfit sanity check on 128 samples, then run a full 5-epoch-per-task replay baseline to re-establish a working baseline and submit eval. Decision: Rewrite model.py (fix pool typo, use F.relu/avg_pool2d), rewrite train.py to ensure buffer logic is complete, keep predict.py, then run sanity check + full training + submit eval.

Observation: Iteration 4 achieved 3.55% average accuracy. Only task 9 has non-zero accuracy (35.5%), confirming catastrophic forgetting and likely a broken replay buffer (previous tasks are exactly 0.0). The custom ResNet-18 can memorize (sanity check passed), but sequential training without effective replay fails completely. Analysis: The previous attempts using a custom ResNet trained end-to-end are too prone to forgetting with tiny buffers, and the buffer code likely had a concatenation bug. Given the compute budget (26 iterations, ~4.7h), I should switch to a much more sample-efficient strategy: use a pre-downloaded pretrained ViT-B/16 as a fixed feature extractor, maintain a class-balanced feature buffer, and train only a lightweight linear head incrementally. This decouples representation learning from classifier learning, making replay extremely effective and training very fast. Decision: Implement a new model.py using timm's pretrained ViT-B/16 (frozen backbone), new train.py that extracts features once per task, stores class-balanced feature exemplars, and trains only the linear head. Run a fast test with 10 epochs per task to validate the pipeline and measure iteration time, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5/6 established a strong baseline of 74.52% average accuracy using a frozen pretrained ViT-B/16 backbone with a replay buffer of 50 feature exemplars per class. Analysis: The model is severely data-limited in the buffer: keeping only 50/≈450 samples per class discards ~89% of training data. Since features are tiny (768-d floats), we can store ALL features (≈45k total ≈ 140 MB) and train the linear head on the full history every task. This is essentially full replay in feature space and should significantly reduce forgetting and improve accuracy. Decision: Rewrite train.py to keep all features (memory_per_class effectively unlimited), use SGD with momentum and weight decay, train 15 epochs per task, and submit eval. Leave model.py and predict.py unchanged.

Observation: Iteration 7 achieved 79.63% average accuracy using a frozen ViT-B/16 backbone with a replay buffer storing all features and training only a linear head sequentially. The training curves show train_acc ~99% but val_acc ~81%, indicating the persistent linear head is overfitting/memorizing and not finding the best joint solution. Analysis: Because the head is fine-tuned incrementally across tasks, it likely settles into a suboptimal minimum. Since we keep ALL past features in the buffer, we can afford to reinitialize the head before training on each task and optimize from scratch on all observed data — this is essentially the joint-training upper bound. Additionally, switching to a cosine classifier (L2-normalized features + no bias) is known to improve continual learning and generalization. Decision: Update model.py to use a cosine linear head (bias=False, L2 normalize features). Rewrite train.py to (1) reinitialize the head and optimizer at the start of every task, (2) train jointly on the full feature buffer, (3) use AdamW with cosine annealing, (4) add label smoothing and light feature-space Gaussian noise for regularization. Run 20 epochs per task (training is very fast since only a linear head is trained on cached features). Then test predict.py locally and submit eval.

python -c "
import os, torch, json
from model import PretrainedViT
ckpt = torch.load('checkpoints/model.pt', map_location='cpu')
print('ckpt keys:', ckpt.keys())
model = PretrainedViT(num_classes=100)
model.head.load_state_dict(ckpt['head'])
val = torch.load(os.path.join(os.environ.get('RAB_DATA_DIR','/data'), 'val.pt'))
with torch.no_grad():
    model.eval()
    out = model(val['images'][:4])
    print('preds', out.argmax(1).tolist())
"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 achieved a new best of 82.43% average accuracy with the frozen IN21k ViT + cosine linear head + full feature replay + per-task head reinitialization. Analysis: The current model works well but there is still a ~8-10% gap between training and validation accuracy, suggesting room for improvement. Before launching longer retraining experiments, I can squeeze extra accuracy from the existing checkpoint with test-time augmentation (TTA). Horizontal flip TTA is standard, free, and often adds 0.5-1% on image classification. Decision: Modify predict.py to average predictions from the original and horizontally-flipped test images, keep the existing checkpoint, run a quick local sanity check, and submit for evaluation. This uses minimal time (~40s) and almost no compute.

Observation: Current best is 83.11% average accuracy using IN21k ViT-B/16 + cosine linear head + full feature replay + per-task head reinitialization + horizontal-flip TTA. Analysis: While the replay strategy is solid, the IN21k ViT features may be suboptimal for CIFAR-100 compared to CLIP ViT-B/16, which is known to yield stronger linear-probe performance on natural image datasets. Switching the frozen backbone to the pre-downloaded CLIP ViT-B/16 (with its correct preprocessing) is the highest-impact change available. Predict.py and train.py should be made backbone-agnostic so the checkpoint records which backbone was used. Decision: Rewrite model.py/train.py/predict.py to support a configurable backbone, train with CLIP ViT-B/16 for 20 epochs per task (same replay recipe), verify predict.py locally, and submit eval.

Training is fast (~6-8 min) since only a linear head is trained on cached features. Decision: Rewrite model.py/train.py completely (previous train.py was truncated) with these changes, train with IN21k, verify predict.py locally, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 14's training run produced a final validation accuracy of only ~64%, a massive drop from the prior best of 83.11%. The training logs show stable but poor convergence, indicating a bug in the rewritten train.py (likely in buffer construction or optimization setup). Analysis: Since the IN21k ViT + cosine head recipe previously worked well (iter 8-10), I must discard the buggy train.py and rewrite a clean, minimal version that faithfully implements: (1) per-task feature extraction, (2) full-feature replay buffer, (3) per-task head reinitialization, (4) AdamW + cosine annealing, (5) label smoothing & light feature noise. This should recover the ~82-83% baseline quickly (~5-6 min for 20 epochs). Decision: Rewrite train.py from scratch with verified logic, run 20 epochs, perform a local predict.py sanity check, and immediately submit for evaluation.

Observation: Iteration 15 achieved only 78.73% average accuracy, a significant drop from the best of 83.11% (iteration 10). Training logs show task 9 train_acc=79.5% and val_acc=79.7%, indicating severe underfitting compared to iteration 8's ~95% train_acc and ~82% val_acc. Analysis: model.py and predict.py appear correct; the regression is almost certainly in train.py (truncated in workspace). The most likely bug is scheduler.step() being called per-batch instead of per-epoch, or another subtle training loop error that prevents proper convergence. Decision: Rewrite train.py completely with clean, verified logic matching the proven recipe: IN21k ViT frozen backbone, cosine linear head, full feature replay, per-task head reinitialization, AdamW lr=1e-3, wd=1e-2, CosineAnnealingLR stepped once per epoch, label smoothing 0.1, feature noise 0.01, 20 epochs per task. Run training, verify predict.py offline, then submit eval to recover the ~83% baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 16 completed training with final val_acc ~79.7%, far below the best eval of 83.11% (iteration 10). The training logs show train_acc plateaued at ~79.5% and loss ~1.63, indicating severe underfitting on a linear head. Analysis: The current train.py (truncated in workspace) almost certainly steps the learning-rate scheduler every batch instead of every epoch, causing the LR to collapse to near-zero after the first few batches and preventing convergence. The successful recipe from iteration 8 used per-epoch scheduling and reached ~82.4% (83.1% with TTA). Decision: Rewrite train.py from scratch with a clean, verified loop: scheduler.step() called once per epoch after train_epoch; keep all other proven hyperparameters (AdamW lr=1e-3, wd=1e-2, cosine annealing, label smoothing 0.1, feature noise 0.01, full feature replay, per-task head reinitialization, IN21k ViT backbone, 20 epochs). Run training, verify predict.py offline, and immediately submit eval to recover the 83% baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 17 again produced only 78.73% average accuracy, far below the best of 83.11%. The PyTorch AdamW training loop has been stuck at ~79% train/val accuracy for four iterations, indicating a persistent optimizer convergence failure (likely LR collapse, bad initialization state, or hidden loop bug). Analysis: Instead of debugging the stalled AdamW loop again, I will switch to a full-batch LBFGS optimizer on cached features. LBFGS is a second-order method that robustly converges for convex problems like linear probing, bypassing all learning-rate scheduling and SGD/AdamW pitfalls. With all 45k feature vectors fitting in GPU memory (~140 MB), I can train the linear head after each task using the complete buffer, reinitializing the head each time. This should find a much better joint solution and recover the 82-83% baseline, potentially exceeding it. Decision: Rewrite train.py to use torch.optim.LBFGS with strong_wolfe line search on the full feature buffer. Keep model.py and predict.py unchanged (cosine head, TTA). Run training + local verification, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 18 achieved 81.89% average accuracy with LBFGS, still below the best of 83.11% obtained in iteration 10 using AdamW + cosine head + full feature replay + per-task head reinit + TTA. Analysis: The LBFGS optimizer converges to near-zero training loss but generalizes worse than the properly-regularized AdamW recipe (label smoothing 0.1, feature noise 0.01, cosine annealing stepped per epoch). The current train.py is the LBFGS version. I need to revert to the proven AdamW recipe, increasing epochs from 20 to 30 to squeeze extra accuracy, while keeping the same IN21k ViT backbone and TTA predict.py. Decision: Rewrite train.py with clean AdamW loop, per-epoch cosine annealing, label smoothing, feature noise, and best-head selection per task. Run training + verify predict.py, then immediately submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 19 achieved only 77.12% average accuracy, far below the best of 83.11% from iteration 10. The recent AdamW attempts (iterations 15-19) all reset the linear head at the start of each task, while the successful 82.43% run (iteration 8) likely used a persistent head. Reinitializing the head destroys old-task decision boundaries and, with only 20-30 epochs per task, the optimizer fails to fully reconverge on the growing buffer (45k samples, 100 classes). Analysis: Iteration 8's training curves show smooth monotonic increases without catastrophic drops at task boundaries, consistent with a persistent head. The LBFGS run (iter 18, 81.89%) also reinitialized the head and fell short. I will revert to the proven recipe: frozen IN21k ViT-B/16 + cosine linear head + full feature replay + persistent head (NO reset per task) + AdamW + cosine annealing + label smoothing + feature noise. The head is never reset; instead the optimizer state is refreshed each task to adapt to new data while preserving learned boundaries. This should recover the ~82-83% baseline. Decision: Rewrite train.py to remove head reset, keep all other hyperparameters identical to the clean iter-19 setup, run 30 epochs per task, verify predict.py, and submit eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py && mkdir -p dummy_test && ln -sf $RAB_DATA_DIR/val.pt dummy_test/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path dummy_test --checkpoint checkpoints/model.pt --output test_out.json && python -c "import json; d=json.load(open('test_out.json')); assert len(d['predictions'])==5000; print('Verification passed, preds:', len(d['predictions']))"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 20 achieved 79.76%, far below the best of 83.11%. The persistent head approach clearly underperforms compared to the successful runs from iterations 8-10. Analysis: The training curve from iter_8 shows train_acc starting high and dropping moderately at task boundaries, which is inconsistent with random head reinitialization (that would drop to ~10%). This suggests iter 8 likely used persistent head OR the head reinitialization in later iterations was broken. More importantly, LBFGS with explicit fresh-head creation got 81.89% (iter 18), proving fresh heads help. The AdamW attempts that got stuck at ~78% probably failed because they called reset_parameters() on a custom CosineLinear module that lacks it, so the head was never actually reset. Decision: Rewrite model.py/train.py/predict.py from scratch with a guaranteed-fresh head each task (instantiate new CosineLinear), full feature replay, AdamW+cosine, label smoothing 0.1, feature noise 0.01, 20 epochs. Save full model state dict in checkpoint for robust offline loading. Keep horizontal-flip TTA in predict.py. Run training, verify predict.py locally on val.pt, then submit eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py && mkdir -p dummy_test && cp /data/val.pt dummy_test/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path dummy_test --checkpoint checkpoints/model.pt --output dummy_out.json && python -c "import json; d=json.load(open('dummy_out.json')); print('num preds:', len(d['predictions']))"

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best is 83.11% from iteration 10. Recent AdamW-based linear-head attempts (iters 14-21) have plateaued around 78-82% due to subtle training-loop instabilities. Analysis: Since the frozen IN21k ViT-B/16 backbone produces excellent features, I can bypass head-optimization entirely by using a non-parametric k-NN classifier in feature space. With all ~45k training features stored in the checkpoint, k-NN with cosine similarity should be robust, suffer zero catastrophic forgetting, and might exceed the linear-head score. It also eliminates learning-rate scheduling bugs. Decision: Implement a pure feature-extraction + k-NN pipeline. train.py will only extract and store all task features (no head training). predict.py will extract test features (with horizontal-flip TTA), then perform weighted k-NN (k=10) by cosine similarity. Run training (~3 min), verify predict.py offline on val.pt, and submit eval. If successful, I can tune k and distance metrics in later iterations.

trajectory.command: python -c "import torch; d=torch.load('/data/val.pt'); torch.save({'images': d['images'][:256]}, 'dummy_test/test.pt')" && python predict.py --data_path dummy_test --checkpoint checkpoints/model.pt --output dummy_out.json && python -c "import json; preds=json.load(open('dummy_out.json'))['predictions']; assert len(preds)==256, f'Expected 256 preds, got {len(preds)}'; print('predict.py OK, preds:', len(preds))"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 22 successfully cached all 45k IN21k ViT features into checkpoints/model.pt for a k-NN classifier. Iteration 23 failed only because a local verification command tried to overwrite dummy_test/test.pt, which is hardlinked to the read-only /data filesystem; the checkpoint itself is intact and predict.py is correct. Analysis: The persistent AdamW linear-head attempts have plateaued around 78-82%, while the proven 83.11% best came from an earlier training run whose checkpoint is lost. The k-NN approach eliminates optimizer/scheduler bugs entirely, uses all training data, and with horizontal-flip TTA should be competitive. Decision: Avoid further local file writes to dodge the read-only hardlink issue and submit the existing k-NN checkpoint for evaluation immediately. If k-NN underperforms, remaining time and iterations are sufficient to retrain a linear head or tune k.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 22's k-NN classifier achieved a new best of 85.38% average accuracy, surpassing all prior linear-head attempts (max 83.11%). Analysis: k-NN in feature space bypasses training instability and leverages all 45k samples. However, the default k=10 and raw cosine-similarity weights may be suboptimal. A quick hyperparameter search over k and a temperature-scaled weighting on the validation set costs no training time and can improve test accuracy. Decision: Write tune_k.py to grid-search k and temperature on val.pt using the existing checkpoint, and update predict.py to dynamically load the best config. Run tuning and submit eval in the same iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: The k-NN classifier with frozen IN21k ViT-B/16 reached a new best of 85.74% average accuracy in iteration 25. Analysis: The current buffer only stores original training features (~45k). By augmenting the buffer with horizontally-flipped training features (~90k total), k-NN gains denser feature-space coverage, which should improve accuracy. Additionally, predict.py currently averages feature vectors before k-NN; switching to prediction-level TTA (averaging k-NN vote scores from original and flipped test views) is more robust. Decision: Rewrite train.py to cache both original and flipped training features, rewrite predict.py for score-level TTA, and rewrite tune_k.py to tune on val with TTA. Run training+tuning, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The k-NN classifier with flipped training features and score-level TTA achieved a new best of 86.41% average accuracy on iteration 26. Analysis: Because the IN21k ViT backbone is frozen, we can further boost accuracy by training a lightweight linear head on the full cached feature buffer (90k samples) after all tasks are collected. This avoids continual-learning optimization instabilities entirely. A linear probe on 768-d features with 900 samples/class should be very strong and can be ensembled with the existing k-NN classifier for a further gain. Decision: Write train_head.py to train a linear head on the cached buffer with AdamW + cosine annealing + label smoothing, evaluate on val, search the best ensemble weight between k-NN and linear head, update predict.py to support the ensemble, verify offline, and immediately submit eval in the same iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 27's train_head.py successfully trained a linear head and found an optimal ensemble weight (w=0.6, val_acc=0.8946) before crashing on a read-only dummy_test file write. The workspace file list includes checkpoints/ensemble_config.json and checkpoints/linear_head.pt, suggesting they were saved before the crash. Analysis: If these files are valid, the k-NN + linear head ensemble should significantly exceed the current best test average accuracy of 86.41%. Decision: Run a lightweight verification command that checks file existence, inspects ensemble_config.json, creates a temp dummy test.pt, runs predict.py, and asserts output length. If this command succeeds, immediately submit_eval with checkpoints/model.pt in the same iteration. If any step fails, the submit_eval will be skipped and I can fix in the next iteration.

trajectory.command: cp checkpoints/model.pt checkpoints/model_best.pt && cp checkpoints/ensemble_config.json checkpoints/ensemble_config_best.json && cp checkpoints/knn_config.json checkpoints/knn_config_best.json && cp checkpoints/linear_head.pt checkpoints/linear_head_best.pt && python train.py && python tune_k.py && python train_head.py && python -c "import json, shutil; new=json.load(open('checkpoints/ensemble_config.json')); old=json.load(open('checkpoints/ensemble_config_best.json')); print('new_val_acc:',new['ensemble_val_acc'],'old_val_acc:',old['ensemble_val_acc']); assert new['ensemble_val_acc']>0.0" && python -c "import json, shutil, os; new=json.load(open('checkpoints/ensemble_config.json')); old=json.load(open('checkpoints/ensemble_config_best.json')); [shutil.copy(f'checkpoints/{n}_best.json',f'checkpoints/{n}.json') for n in ['ensemble_config','knn_config']] or shutil.copy('checkpoints/model_best.pt','checkpoints/model.pt') or shutil.copy('checkpoints/linear_head_best.pt','checkpoints/linear_head.pt') if new['ensemble_val_acc'] <= old['ensemble_val_acc'] else None; print('Kept new' if new['ensemble_val_acc'] > old['ensemble_val_acc'] else 'Restored old')" && mkdir -p /tmp/dummy_test && python -c "import torch,os; vd=torch.load(os.path.join(os.environ.get('RAB_DATA_DIR','/data'),'val.pt')); torch.save({'images':vd['images'][:100]},'/tmp/dummy_test/test.pt')" && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/dummy_test --checkpoint checkpoints/model.pt --output /tmp/dummy_out.json && python -c "import json; d=json.load(open('/tmp/dummy_out.json')); assert len(d['predictions'])==100; print('predict.py OK, predictions:',d['predictions'][:10])"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 28 achieved a new best of 89.79% average accuracy with an ensemble of k-NN and a linear head on frozen IN21k ViT features, using original+flip training features (90k) and TTA. Analysis: The buffer currently discards ~75% of training data by keeping only two views per image. Since the backbone is frozen, we can cheaply densify the buffer with more augmented views (random crop + flip) to improve both k-NN coverage and linear head training. Adding 2 extra augmented views (4x total) increases the buffer to ~180k feature vectors (~550 MB) but extraction time remains under 2 minutes. This should boost k-NN accuracy and give the linear head more diverse data, potentially pushing the ensemble past 90%. To limit risk, I will back up the old best checkpoint/configs, run the augmented pipeline, compare the new ensemble validation accuracy to the old 0.8946, and restore the old checkpoint if the new one is worse before submitting. Decision: Rewrite train.py to cache 4 views per image (orig, flip, 2x random crop+flip) using kornia, rewrite train_head.py to add mixup regularization, then run backup -> train -> tune k -> train head -> conditional restore -> offline verify -> submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 29 overwrote checkpoints/model.pt with a 4x-augmented buffer that performed worse (85.63% vs the best 89.79% from iteration 28). The old ensemble configs may have been restored but model.pt remained corrupted. I have 1 iteration left and ~2.1 hours. Analysis: The proven recipe from iter 28 (89.79%) used: frozen IN21k ViT-B/16, original+flip training features (2x), k-NN (k=50, T=0.05), linear head trained with mixup/AdamW/cosine, and score-level TTA ensemble (w=0.6). To recover the best score, I must regenerate the checkpoint with exactly 2x features, then re-run k tuning, head training, and ensemble search. Decision: Rewrite model.py, train.py (2x only), and train_head.py (complete, non-truncated). Keep predict.py and tune_k.py. Run the full pipeline, verify predict.py on a /tmp dummy test, and immediately submit eval.
